title: Is This Website Real? Spotting Fakes

modules.title: Module 1: How AI Generates Convincing Fake Sites, Brands, and Storefronts
modules.sub: Understanding how quickly and cheaply AI builds deceptive websites
modules.description: Learners explore the specific AI tools and techniques scammers use to clone real brands, generate fictional storefronts, and fabricate convincing identities at scale. By seeing the process from the attacker's perspective, learners build intuition for why traditional red flags are no longer enough. This context sets the foundation for the verification routine introduced throughout the rest of the course.

modules.title: Module 2: Reading URLs, Certificates, and Domain Age — What They Actually Tell You
modules.sub: Decoding the address bar before you trust any website
modules.description: Learners practice dissecting URLs to spot lookalike domains, typosquatting tricks, and misleading subdomains that impersonate legitimate sites. They also learn what HTTPS certificates actually guarantee — and what they don't — so they stop treating the padlock icon as a safety signal. Domain age checks are introduced as a quick first filter for flagging recently registered sites.

modules.title: Module 3: Reverse Image Search, WHOIS Lookup, and Free Verification Tools
modules.sub: Using accessible tools to investigate any suspicious website
modules.description: Learners walk through a hands-on toolkit of free resources — including reverse image search, WHOIS registrar lookups, and site-reputation checkers — and practice applying them to real-world examples. They make judgment calls about what the data reveals and when findings should raise concern. By the end, each learner has a personal checklist of go-to tools they can bookmark and use immediately.

modules.title: Module 4: Red Flags in AI-Generated Content — Reviews, Bios, and Product Descriptions
modules.sub: Spotting telltale patterns in AI-written text and fake social proof
modules.description: Learners analyze examples of AI-generated reviews, employee bios, and product descriptions, learning to recognize linguistic patterns, implausible specificity, and suspiciously uniform praise. They practice distinguishing authentic user-generated content from fabricated copy designed to manufacture trust. This module sharpens the critical reading skills that complement the technical tools covered elsewhere in the course.

modules.title: Module 5: What to Do When You've Already Clicked — Damage Control Steps
modules.sub: Taking fast, effective action after a potential phishing or scam encounter
modules.description: Learners work through a prioritized response sequence for common scenarios — entering credentials, submitting payment information, or downloading a file from a suspicious site. They identify which accounts and institutions to contact first, how to document the incident, and when to escalate to consumer protection agencies. The goal is to replace panic with a calm, practiced routine that limits harm.

modules.title: Module 6: Teaching This Skill to Others — Family, Coworkers, and Community
modules.sub: Translating your verification habits into guidance others can use
modules.description: Learners adapt the five-point verification routine for different audiences — including older relatives, non-technical coworkers, and community members — choosing language and examples that resonate without overwhelming. They draft a short explainer or talking-points guide they could share in a real conversation or group setting. This module reinforces personal mastery while expanding protective impact beyond the individual learner.

modules.title: Module 7: Staying Current as Fake Sites Get Smarter
modules.sub: Building habits and sources to keep pace with evolving scam tactics
modules.description: Learners identify reliable sources — threat intelligence feeds, consumer watchdog sites, and security newsletters — that surface emerging fake-site techniques without requiring technical expertise to follow. They design a simple personal review habit, such as a monthly five-minute check of new scam trends, to keep their skills calibrated over time. The module closes by reinforcing that verification is an ongoing practice, not a one-time lesson.

synopsis: AI tools have made it trivially cheap to clone a legitimate website, fabricate reviews, and register convincing domains — and shoppers, job seekers, and patients are losing money and data to these fakes every day. This course gives everyday users a repeatable, tool-supported routine for checking whether a website is trustworthy before they click, buy, or submit personal information.
rationale: AI-generated phishing sites and fake storefronts have exploded in volume and quality, outpacing the visual instincts most users developed against older, cruder fakes.
learning_outcome: Learners can apply a consistent five-point verification routine to any unfamiliar website and use free tools to confirm or flag its legitimacy before sharing data or money.
source_signals: How To Check If A Website Is Legit Or Scam?
New Scams to Watch Out For in 2025
